paragraphs.body: All the Unspoken Words

The night Taran turned 21, as the guests chatted away, he made an excuse to use the restroom and slipped away into his mother’s bedroom. There, he grabbed the bottles stored away that were supposed to celebrate him but would celebrate her. Pop, he held the first one, his grip tightening and his heart thundering. He went to the bathroom, and hesitated for just a moment before plsshh down the toilet bowl it went. He flushed. Then rinse and repeat, popping open the bottle, pouring it down the toilet, and flushing. Until finally, his mother’s cabinet, the one she could not live without, was empty.

annotations.comment: I’m extremely interested in this action and it’s a vivid way to begin the story. I can’t tell if the bottles are champagne or pills, though. (Or something else?)

paragraphs.body: His hands twitched, wanting to tear apart the cabinet, but he knew he couldn’t. What he had done was already going to make his life hell for at least the next three months, he couldn’t risk anything that would make things worse. He slipped back downstairs, hiding his face. No one noticed.

annotations.comment: Again, fascinating but I don’t have quite enough information.

paragraphs.body: It was the first time Taran was going to be home alone. He couldn’t wait to be back and get into his video games as soon as his butt hit the couch. It was about to be the best day of his life. But when he unlocked the front door and slipped in, there his mother was, slumped on the chair. In her hand was a glass, the bourbon about an eighth way up. How is her grip still strong enough to hold the glass? he had thought to himself, right before it fell to the ground and shattered.

But still, his mother did not wake up. Instead, her fingers flexed inwards and then loosened again before she turned, her eyes undoubtedly still closed. He stood there, not knowing what to do. His mother would drink here and there, whiskey and beer no strangers to his home. But he had never seen her like this before.

He dialed his grandmother’s number on the landline, the ten digits were listed on the emergency contacts sheet his mother had written out the night before even though he had had it memorized since he was six. His grandmother was calm but authoritative, walking him through the steps to help his mother. He laid her on her side on the floor and tried to ignore his heartbeat to listen to her breathing. Eventually, his grandmother came and soon after, the medics. Each moment, from the rush to the hospital to the next few days, was an eternity.

annotations.comment: It’s a nice detail that he expected to be alone and she prepped him for that.

paragraphs.body: She was supposed to have been at work that day. She hadn’t been able to find a babysitter for him in time and when she asked his grandmother, his grandmother had vehemently refused, stating that now Taran was old enough to look after himself.

annotations.comment: But here, I got confused — wasn’t Taran prepped to be left alone? I’m not sure how this paragraph wraps up this part of the story.

paragraphs.body: The guests slowly trickled out until finally, the place was empty save for him and his mother. And now with no one there to be her distraction, she finally looked at him, her face somehow always sad whenever her eyes landed on him. “Happy Birthday, dear,” she said, her voice soft.

She was good at hiding it but he was an expert at detecting the signs, what with the redness in her eyes and all. His eyes trailed down her arm until they landed on the confirmation: a cup of Scotch she must have grabbed when he was upstairs and somehow hidden when he got back. He didn’t say anything, just watched as her hands trembled a bit.

annotations.comment: Interesting, because she didn’t seem to be hiding her drunken state earlier.

paragraphs.body: They stared at each other, almost like it was a game. A game no one ever won.
“I’m going for a drive,” he said. She didn’t try stopping him. She had given up a long time ago.

annotations.comment: Just noting that you’ve got a character here with a problem — an alcoholic mom (and maybe some inner turmoil too) — but I don’t yet see this character in a conflict, where he wants something he can’t have. The potential is there — him vs. his mom. But when you have one of them unconscious or leaving the scene, it’s hard to keep that conflict going.

paragraphs.body: Each time it was the same, a pattern that he couldn’t ever be free of. Maybe it was time to leave, she could handle herself. What was he doing still living with his mother at the ripe old age of 21?

annotations.comment: I was surprised here, as 21 isn’t that old!

paragraphs.body: “Come on,” Avon said, his grin leery. The lights were flashing, giving Taran a headache. He thought it’d be fun to let loose for once but he was starting to sorely regret that decision. “Just one sip.”

annotations.text: He thought it’d be fun to let loose for once but he was starting to sorely regret that decision.
annotations.comment: This really told me a lot.

paragraphs.body: “No.”

People typically listened. They respected, to at least a small degree, boundaries. But not Avon. He kept begging until, eventually, Taran said a short “fine” and grabbed the shot glass. One small drink couldn’t hurt right? His stomach turned inwards and he looked down at it. If it would get Avon to stop being annoying for a little bit, it might be worth it. And besides, Taran wasn’t his mother. Sure, she might have given birth to him and sure, he shared 50% of her genes but it was just 50%.

annotations.text: Sure, she might have given birth to him and sure, he shared 50% of her genes but it was just 50%.
annotations.comment: I would cut this line, but I like this scene. It came a live for me. I did see him struggling against his mom here.

paragraphs.body: Taran took it down, the taste burning first his tongue, then the back of his throat until he could swallow. He winced, shaking his head. The burning ached for a good minute until there were remnants of lightness. Slowly, the light turned to warmth and spread throughout his stomach and up to his chest. It made him giddy and happy, a genuine smile spreading across his lips. It felt good, too good.
And he got it, at least primitively, why his mother always chose the bottles over him.
The tug of war of his deep desire to leave everything behind and his fierce need to protect his mother consumed him.

“Go out there,” his mother smiled the night he confessed his desire to go to school in California, “They would love to have you. Of course I’ll miss you but you belong to the coast three hours behind.” She sniffed and hugged him. A few hours later, she had slipped into an abyss, her door locked, and Taran breaking the doorknob to get to her.

annotations.comment: One aspect of the story I’m finding confusing is when the italics are deployed. It seemed, at first, that you used them to show a scene in the past. But this section appears to be set in the past, and there aren’t any italics.
The hint of an exciting scene we don’t get to read.

paragraphs.body: Taran’s grip tightened on the wheel, his foot incidentally pressing down on the acceleration. It was time to move on. He was almost done with college and he’d be looking for jobs soon. California had been out of reach then but it didn’t have to mean that it would still be out of reach. His mother was dealing with this BS for almost ten years now. There was a limit.

annotations.comment: I was surprise that Taran doesn’t have a more compassionate way of viewing his mom’s alcoholism than this. I get that she has ruined much for him (and that’s sad!) but it seemed strange that he has no understanding of the condition as a disease.

paragraphs.body: He blinked away tears and reached into the compartment his mother hid her flask in. Grabbing it, he drank the last couple of sips and felt that lovely warmth spread through.

That morning marked the five year anniversary of Arun’s death. Sudesh woke up content. Arun had been the love of her life, a gentle soul that had enraptured her since the beginning. She thought about him every moment, her chest full of so much love. Any anger she had felt towards the driver of the car that had killed Arun had long turned to forgiveness. Arun would have wanted her to be with Taran, her wonderful grandson. And she herself didn’t want to spend another moment in the lonely apartment that had been empty without Arun.

annotations.comment: I had a little trouble following who these folks are.

paragraphs.body: Kyathi had been trying to convince Sudesh to move in with her but there was too much of Arun to leave it. The apartment was too far from the school Taran attended for them to move in with her. Once she completed the prayers that celebrated Arun’s memory and it was around the time Taran was to be back from school, she grabbed her keys. Ideally, Kyathi and Taran would have been there for the prayers but Kyathi always behaved quite strangely when Sudesh asked so Sudesh had decided not to push.

Taran would probably be parked in front of the TV, not caring at all about the list of chores Kyathi had set aside for him. He loved having Sudesh as his gaming partner, her fingers quick on the joysticks and buttons of the controller even at 65 years old. Right as Sudesh got to the car, she received a call.

Later, Sudesh tried explaining to Taran, “Sometimes grief shows up in the strangest ways at the strangest times. It’s been five years since your mom lost her father and she’s still grieving,” but his eyes were fixated to his left, away from where Kyathi laid. She wasn’t sure what he was thinking so she held him close. She sent up a quick prayer, told Taran stories about Arun and Kyathi, and rubbed circles on his back.

annotations.comment: I was pretty lost here. Sorry!

paragraphs.body: Taran wiped his mouth and set the flask onto the cupholder. The dizziness swayed him a little so he tightened his grip on the wheels further, squinting his eyes to focus on the road. His phone rang, it was his mother. She had probably finally meandered up to her room and seen the empty cabinet. He smiled, imagining how worried and angry she must be. Not at his disappearance but at her beloved’s disappearance.
She was probably done with him but it didn’t matter because he was done too. Tomorrow, he was going to get out of here. Hell, he’d buy the plane tickets and transfer elsewhere.
He took a breath, looked at his hands for a moment to ensure his fingers slid properly on the touch screen, and picked up the call.
“Taran—”

He heard the screech of metal on metal first before he saw the white, bright lights that seared into his eyes. At that moment, he thought to himself how interesting that was because the speed of light was faster than the speed of sound.

annotations.comment: Scarlett, I enjoyed the complexity of this draft — your use of switchback time is effective for a story like this, and I enjoyed much of the specificity. I had trouble, at times, following the conflict, as I noted above. I think the idea is that Taran wants to punish his mother for her drinking, perhaps? But she is often unconscious or not in the scene with him making it hard to see his anger towards her develop. At the same time, you have him begin drinking as well, and then, tragically, die in a DUI of some kind. That does seem to punish the mother, in a broad way, so he gets what he wanted. But without seeing her receive the news of his death it didn’t feel like a satisfying ending. Don’t despair — there’s a reason why I said switchback time is the hardest to write! I wonder what happens if you think about this story as a series of scenes from the past and the present. In the past, we see Taran try to get his mother to stop drinking and/or care about him. (We probably don’t need the confusing grandparent scene, therefore.) And in the present, we see him decide to drink, drink, and face the punishment for it. Maybe you can show that the mom is the trigger for his drinking — she calls or something? You always write well and with a lot of good insight. You’ve chosen complicated topic and structure here. Try focusing in on your actual plot and conflict in this next draft - it will help!
